Okay, the impossible balance. What you must accept and understand is that leadership, there is no black and white and there is no right or wrong. Humans are complicated, businesses are complicated, situations are complicated, life is complicated. And so you can only do your best at any given point in time to balance your demonstration of micromanaging versus letting others take complete ownership. There are going to be times, a lot of them, probably every day, when you lean too far one way or the other. Okay, and this is something you'll become aware of. Because of this, because of you leaning one way or the other, I want you to understand people will lose their jobs because of it, you will lose customers because of it, and you will lose money because of it. Okay, all because you held on too tight or you let go too much. And this is something that you need to accept rather than reject and demand perfection of Because perfect does not exist in the scenarios because they are subject to each person. Okay, so each scenario that deals with a person, each person in that scenario has a different idea of what perfection looks like. And it's especially different depending on your role. So there's no point trying to aim for that because it's not going to happen. And so many CEOs and leaders come in and they start in a role in the beginning, their first business or their first time in leadership, and they're really good at one or the other, but they're not good at both. And what you have to do in order to grow as a leader is learn the skill in the area you lack for the good of your team in the business. There's a lot of times where people, and a lot of people will tell you, don't focus on anything you're not good at. In leadership, there are skills that if you lack and if you ignore your lack of competence, they will kill your business. And this is one of them. I want you to understand that. If you do not learn this balance, if you understand only micromanaging or only letting go, then you will not become a successful leader. You have to understand both. Okay. So what I want to talk about is wartime versus peacetime because this will teach you when it is appropriate to demonstrate micromanaging versus letting others take ownership. Okay. What is peacetime? Peacetime is when your company has a large advantage in the marketplace. Competition is low. Okay. You've just entered a new area. There's not much competition around you. Nobody's doing what you're doing right now, et cetera. The market is growing and thriving. So there's no shortage of customers. Growth is just beginning. And if anything, you are accommodating for it to do your best to keep up. Meaning you can barely keep up with the growth. You can barely fulfill on the back end because there's just so much coming in from the front end, right? Leaders maximize the current opportunity and leaders encourage creativity and exploration of new options. Meaning it's a time where you can encourage people to explore their skill sets, explore testing what they're good at in your business. Where do they like? Are they creative with this? Do they like making ads? Do they like following up with customers, like switching them around and really helping nurture them, right? Wartime. The company is dealing with third party threats, market trends, platforms changing, user behavior changing. Two, competition is high and the market is saturated with options. I would say there's no business nowadays. Almost zero businesses because of the boom of the internet 15 years ago that doesn't have high competition. Three, single focus, which means you need to throw out new ideas and creativity because you need one single focus during wartime. And then four is companies survival is really dependent upon the ability to focus on that single thing. All right, so it's imperative that during this time people are not throwing their ideas out there. They're not like, why are we doing this? So I had this really great deal. You're like, no, we're executing the plan. And so you must be able to fluctuate your management style based on where a company sits in the market. So what does that look like? How do you act during these different times? Here's what a peacetime leader looks like. You follow proper protocol because it leads to winning. You focus on the big picture, allow the details to be flexible. There's room for error. You're like, I don't get it. If you mess up, it's fine. We'll fix it. Don't worry about it. You spend time defining culture. You pour a ton into your people. You nurture them. You have a lot of contingency plans. So there's a lot of things going on. There's a lot of projects in the mix. You're like, these are the five things we're working on right now. You speak kindly and you're always mindful of how you appear to the team. You always only be building them up. Make sure that you're appearing as a great steady leader. You tolerate deviations from the plan, meaning if we have to go this way or that way or this is our new goal right now, you're like, it's cool because things are going well and like overall we're fine. You minimize work conflicts, meaning you're much more mindful of how you speak to people, the conflicts that are occurring, that kind of stuff. You set big exciting goals. So really growth goals, stretch goals, that kind of stuff. You train to meet employee satisfaction, meaning that you gear a lot of how you train people towards helping them explore themselves, helping them understand where their best place in the company is, helping equip them for the next job they're going to have. And then there's a wartime leader. A wartime leader violates protocol to win the game for the business, meaning if your sales guy is sucking and you ain't got any money to lose, then you go in and sell. And he's like, well, why would you not train me on this? Because it's time to fricking win. And like either I can sit here and be patient with you and we can lose the business or I can come in and sell some shit and then train you later. Right? It's for the good of the business. Focus on any and all details if they slow down the win. This is when you have higher expectations of people. This is when your tolerance is low. You'll kind of mess up because there's more on the line. Instead of defining the culture, you let the war define it. What do I mean by that? Whatever it is that you're battling against right now, whatever the existential crisis is, that's what's going to define the team. That's what's going to train and shape the team. You're not training and shaping them through Zoom calls during this time. You're training and shaping them by the execution. Okay? You're not sugarcoating anything to the team. They know it's wartime. You're obvious about it. You're telling them, hey, no new ideas. We're executing what's going on right now. Right? You're intolerant of slight deviation from the plan, meaning like if somebody steers off track of one person out of the seven people you have dropped the ball, you don't tolerate it. You're like, this is unacceptable. Okay? You come in and you micromanage. You don't explore or tolerate naysayers. There's no time for people to disagree with inside the business because there's disagreements and existential threats outside the business. Okay? All you need is people who are naysaying you within the business. You train the employees to survive the battle. What do you do? You don't train them based on their future self and growth and things like that. You train them of like, this is what we have right now and we need to conquer this enemy right now. That's how they're trained and that's how they're shaped. You need to identify where your business sits and then what kind of leader you need to be during that time. That's the best thing you can do for your company. That's the best thing you can do for your team. A lot of people really struggle with this because they feel that they go far too far one way or too far the other. I feel the best way to look at it is to look at what does the business need right now? What kind of leader does the business need you to be? It's going to be peacetime or wartime. Okay? You need to learn what the rules are and then you also need to learn when to break them.
